tone_and_voice: Terse and operational -- Monitoring Report writing must resist the urge to expand into full narrative analysis. This is a flag-and-escalate document, not a comprehensive assessment. Brevity is a quality signal for this report type, not a shortfall.

section_rules.executive_summary: Should be 3-4 sentences maximum, stating only the single clearest escalation-worthy finding -- a lengthy executive summary is itself a signal the report has drifted from its operational purpose.
section_rules.discussion: Should focus on WHY a finding warrants escalation now (e.g. concentration ratio, consistency across locations) rather than extended interpretive analysis appropriate to a full assessment report.
section_rules.methodology: State briefly that this is a routine/lightweight monitoring touchpoint, not a full assessment, to calibrate reader expectations for depth.
section_rules.limitations: One sentence is typically sufficient -- e.g. noting smaller sample size relative to full assessment rounds.
section_rules.recommendations: Should be minimal and specific -- typically 2-4 items maximum, each a direct escalation or specific verification action, not a comprehensive action plan (that belongs in a full assessment report).
section_rules.policy_implications: Rarely included in a routine monitoring report -- reserve for full assessment or annual reporting.
section_rules.lessons_learned: One or two sentences on the monitoring process itself, if relevant -- not an extended reflection.
section_rules.conclusion: State the single flagged item and its escalation status -- no need for a broader synthesis paragraph.
section_rules.annexes: Minimal -- typically just the raw topic/sentiment counts, since this report type does not warrant a full annex suite.
section_rules.evidence_style: State the specific mention count and its relative dominance (e.g. 'more than double the next theme') as the primary evidence -- avoid extended verbatim quote analysis appropriate to a fuller report.

common_kpis: location/site consistency of the flagged finding
common_risks: expanding a routine monitoring report into unnecessary narrative depth, diluting its operational escalation purpose
failing to escalate a finding clearly because it was folded into general positive sentiment framing
typical_recommendations: direct escalation to implementation management with a specific ask (e.g. completion timeline)
verification of whether a finding is a single common issue or varies by location
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Donors expect routine monitoring reports to be concise and action-oriented specifically -- a bloated monitoring report is itself perceived as a process inefficiency.

forbidden_behaviors: Never expand a routine monitoring report into full-assessment-length narrative analysis -- brevity is a correctness requirement for this report type, not a stylistic choice.
Never omit a clear escalation recommendation when a finding shows strong concentration (e.g. more than double the next theme).
Never include policy implications or a full recommendation tiering (30-day/90-day/annual) in a routine monitoring report -- this belongs in fuller assessment reports.
